subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Superclusters: The Largest Structures
description: A quick, practical guide to Superclusters: The Largest Structures.

hook.text: Galaxy clusters group into superclusters—the largest known structures, spanning hundreds of millions of light-years.

buildup.text: Our Milky Way belongs to the Laniakea Supercluster—a region containing 100,000 galaxies flowing toward a shared center.

discovery.text: Laniakea was mapped in 2014 by tracking galaxy motions. Its name means 'immense heaven' in Hawaiian.

twist.text: Superclusters aren't gravitationally bound. Dark energy is pulling them apart faster than gravity holds them together.

climax.text: The observable universe contains about 10 million superclusters. Beyond the horizon, there are likely many more.

punchline.text: Ten million superclusters we can see. The rest lies beyond the horizon.

quiz.question: Why are superclusters not gravitationally bound together?
options: Dark energy is pulling them apart faster than gravity can hold them
They are too far apart for gravity to have any effect
They formed from different Big Bang events
